text: Chapter 2, Gold from the Sky. Answers came from every direction, most of them useless. It exploded up there. No, it rose out of Miller's Gulch like a devil kettle. I seen wings on it. You did not, did too. Dust held up one hand. The town quieted just enough for one person to be heard properly. That person turned out to be Miss Letty Vale, owner of the Telegraph Office, who always talked as if every sentence should win a ribbon. "Marshall," she said. The stagecoach appeared at sunrise. It drifted in from the east. Then the sacks began falling. Then those three roof-climbing coyotes arrived and started claiming the coach belonged to them. Dust looked toward the saloon roof, where the masked men were still trying to lasso air with no visible improvement in skill. Did they say who they ride for? One of them yelled the name Black Jack Varn before he slipped on the shingles. That got Dust's attention. Black Jack Varn was no ordinary outlaw. He was a thief, a gambler, a tinkerer, and the only man in three territories arrogant enough to sign his crimes like they were invitations to a dance. He had once robbed a mining office using fake survey papers. He had once escaped jail in a collapsible coffin with breathing holes. And six months ago, he had been heard bragging in tombstone that horses were too slow and roads were for ordinary mines. Dust glanced up at the coach again. "That sounds like Varn." The mayor tugged on her sleeve. "Can you shoot it down?" "Sure," Dust said. "If you want two thousand stolen coins ringing through your rooftops and whatever fool built that machine landing on the schoolhouse." "No, then I won't!" Another sack tumbled from the coach and burst in the street. Coins whirled between boots and wagon wheels. Half the crowd lunged at once. The banker made a sound like a tea kettle in pain. Dust fired one shot into the air. Everyone froze. "Nobody keeps a single coin," she said. "Anything with bankmarks goes back in the sacks. If I catch you stuffing your pockets, you'll be sweeping horse stalls till your grandkids complain about it." There were grumbles, but there was also Dust Calloway's revolver, which had settled many arguments before. As the townspeople bent to gather the silver, Dust studied the drifting coach. It was too steady to be a balloon and too noisy to be magic. She could hear a faint clatter under the wheat, metal hitting metal in a rhythm almost like a locomotive trying not to cough. "Steem," she mumbled. Miss Letty heard her. "You think it flies with steam? I think Blackjack Vorn found a way to bolt too many bad ideas together." Right then, Bishop jerked hard against the hitch-rail and gave a loud, angry bray. Dust-toned. The mule was gnarled. looking at the sky. He was looking at the water tower at the far end of town. Three men were creeping around its base with shovels. Not the same three on the saloon roof. These wore dust scarves and moved with the nervous speed of men doing something they hoped no one noticed. Dust narrowed her eyes. "Mayor?" "Yes?" "How much do you like your water tower?" The mayor blinked. "A normal amount? You better go love it from up close." She started toward the tower at one. The mayor came stumbling after her, shouting for deputies who either did not exist or had developed a sudden interest in staying out of sight. By the time dust reached the tower, the three men were already backing away from a fresh patch of toned dirt. One saw her and yelled, "One!" All three bolted. Dust didn't chase them. She dropped one million brushed dirt aside with fourth hands. There it was. A wall of dynamite sticks wired together and tucked under the cower supports. "Well now," she said. The mayor arrived panting so hard he could barely stand. "Is that what I think it is?" "Depends," said Dust. "If you think it's picnic handles, no." The mayor would pale. Dust looked back up at the floating stagecoach, then at the dynamite. Then at the panicked town gathering silver in the street. Blackjack Vaughan had not lost control. He had made a plan. The coach dropped the loot. The town rushed to grab it. Then the water tower blew. Chaos took over and the gang escaped with whatever mattered most. Dust stood. "Mayor, get buckets, blankets, and anybody with steady hands. Nobody touches this noose till I say so. What are you doing?" Dust checked the sink on her saddle. Then untied Bishop. "Going up."
